steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is an Inferences question. The decisive clues are: (1) people perceive meaningful sounds as quieter than meaningless sounds at the same volume; (2) participants likely treated the nature sounds as noise (meaningless). Applied to *Heartbeats* (meaningful music) vs. nature sounds (meaningless noise) both at 61.5 dB, the participant would perceive the nature sounds as louder even though the actual volume is identical.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — adjusted the volume of the nature sounds to be greater than 61.5 decibels.: the study says perception differs, not that participants would raise the actual volume of nature sounds. This confuses perceived loudness with physical adjustment. ✗
  • Choice B — believed that neither the nature sounds nor *Heartbeats* were at a volume of 61.5 decibels.: too broad. The participant would perceive one as quieter and one as louder, not both as simply "not 61.5 dB." Nothing in the passage supports dismissing both. ✗
  • Choice C — experienced the nature sounds as louder than *Heartbeats* even though they were not.: directly applies the finding. Meaningful music (*Heartbeats*) is perceived as quieter; meaningless noise (nature sounds) is perceived as louder — even at identical actual volume. ✓
  • Choice D — perceived the nature sounds and *Heartbeats* to be comparably meaningful despite perceiving the music to be quieter.: contradicts the passage. Participants treated nature sounds as meaningless (noise), so they would not perceive both sounds as comparably meaningful. ✗
That matches Choice C — experienced the nature sounds as louder than *Heartbeats* even though they were not..
